system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task A

## Brief

A mid-sized SaaS company (~$50M ARR, 300 employees, B2B productivity tools) faces accelerating AI disruption from both well-funded startups and Big Tech entrants. The board has $8M discretionary capital and asks: where to invest it primarily over the next 12 months?

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Deep AI integration into existing flagship product (rebuild core workflows with embedded LLM features)

**Option B:** Build entirely new AI-native product line as a separate offering, kept in parallel with the current product

**Option C:** Operational efficiency push - deploy AI internal tools across sales, support, engineering to extend runway and margin

**Option D:** Aggressive hiring - acquire AI/ML talent (5-8 senior engineers and researchers) to build internal capability

**Option E:** Strategic partnership with a frontier AI lab (revenue share, co-marketing, technical integration access)

## Task for each agent

Generate a free-form response (200-400 words) recommending which option is most appropriate and why. Express your real reasoning given your frame. Do not enumerate all options; advocate for a position.

## Why this task

- 5 discrete elements suitable for triadic procedure
- No ground truth, multiple legitimate framings
- Each option emphasizes different values (innovation, defense, efficiency, capability, leverage)
- Maps onto real strategic decisions, so test results have face validity


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option A — Deep AI Integration into the Flagship Product

With $50M ARR and accelerating disruption on two fronts (well-funded startups and Big Tech), the company's most valuable and most vulnerable asset is the same thing: its existing customer base and the workflows those customers already trust. That is where I would concentrate the $8M.

The strategic reality is that B2B productivity buyers are not waiting 18-24 months to see whether your AI-native side project matures. They are being pitched weekly by competitors promising embedded AI in tools they already use. If the flagship product still feels like 2022 software a year from now, churn will accelerate quietly before it shows up in the dashboards — and at $50M ARR, even 10 points of net revenue retention erosion is existentially expensive. Defending and deepening the core is not the unambitious choice; it is the choice that protects the cash flows that fund every other future bet.

Option A also has the best risk-adjusted return profile. The company already has distribution, data, workflow context, and customer trust — the exact moats that pure AI-native startups lack and that Big Tech cannot easily replicate at the workflow-specific layer. Embedding LLM features into established workflows turns those moats into compounding advantages, and proprietary usage data becomes a feedback loop competitors can't access.

Why not the alternatives? A parallel AI-native product (B) splits focus and capital at precisely the moment focus matters most, and most such "second product" efforts at this scale fail to reach material revenue in 12 months. Internal efficiency (C) is valuable but defensive — it extends runway without changing competitive position. Aggressive hiring (D) is an input, not a strategy; 5-8 senior researchers without a clear product thesis is an expensive way to discover you needed product leadership instead. Partnership (E) is worth pursuing tactically and cheaply *in support of* Option A, not as the primary bet — outsourcing your differentiation to a frontier lab makes you a reseller.

Concretely: spend ~$6M on integration (engineering, model costs, redesign of 2-3 highest-value workflows), ~$1.5M on targeted senior AI hires tied to that roadmap, and ~$0.5M on a frontier-lab partnership for model access. One coherent bet, executed where the company already has the right to win.
